title: A Guide to Canada's 'Departure Tax' on Emigration

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.bio: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Conduct a 'deemed disposition audit' of all non-registered assets at least a year before departure to forecast your capital gains tax liability and strategically time any actual sales.
Liquidate your Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) before severing residency, as its tax-free status is not recognized abroad and will create a tax liability in your new country.
To prevent double taxation, meticulously document the fair market value from your Canadian deemed disposition as the new official cost basis for your assets in your new country of residence.

Provide indisputable proof of your departure to the CRA by systematically severing residential ties, including closing bank accounts, cancelling provincial health cards, and updating all legal addresses.

Engage a cross-border tax professional early to validate your strategy and navigate the tax laws of both Canada and your destination country, especially for complex assets like stock options or private shares.
summary: Canada's "departure tax" is not a fee but a tax on capital gains from a "deemed disposition," where you are treated as having sold most assets upon ceasing residency, creating a potentially significant liability. To manage this, you must proactively treat your exit as a strategic project, starting with a comprehensive audit of your assets well in advance to model outcomes and make informed decisions about selling or deferring payment. This structured approach transforms compliance anxiety into financial control, allowing you to mitigate your tax burden, prevent double taxation, and begin your new global life with confidence.
ai_overview_answer:
Canada's "departure tax" is not a fee for leaving the country, but an income tax on capital gains from assets that are "deemed disposed" of when you cease to be a Canadian resident for tax purposes. On your departure date, the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) treats you as if you sold most of your assets at their fair market value, triggering a capital gain or loss. This tax generally applies to assets like stocks, mutual funds, and cryptocurrency in non-registered accounts. Key exemptions include Canadian real estate, registered accounts like RRSPs, and funds within a TFSA.

To comply, you must sever residential ties and report the deemed disposition on your final tax return using Form T1243. If the tax owing is significant, you can elect to defer payment by providing adequate security to the CRA. After leaving, it is critical to use the fair market value from your departure as the new cost base for your assets in your new country to prevent double taxation. A notable exemption from the tax exists for "short-term residents" on property they owned before moving to Canada.

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.description: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

step.name: Phase 1: Your 12-Month Pre-Departure Strategic Review
step.text: Your first move is to build a data-driven picture of your potential departure tax liability. Conduct a 'Deemed Disposition Audit' by creating a master ledger of every non-registered asset subject to the departure tax, documenting its acquisition date, adjusted cost base (ACB), and current fair market value. Scenario-model complex assets like employee stock options, cryptocurrency, and private corporation shares to understand their specific tax treatments and timing implications. Strategically time any actual asset sales to utilize available capital losses or tax credits. Finally, decide whether to pay the tax by the deadline or elect to defer the payment by providing adequate security to the CRA, modeling the cash-flow implications of each choice.

step.name: Phase 2: The 90-Day Departure Execution Checklist
step.text: In the final three months, focus on flawless execution. Engage a cross-border tax professional with expertise in both Canada and your destination country. Obtain formal, defensible valuations for any assets without a clear public market price, such as private shares. Liquidate your TFSA, as its tax-free status is not recognized abroad. Prepare your final filing package, including Form T1243 (Deemed Disposition) and Form T1161 (List of Properties). Systematically sever all residential ties by selling or renting your home, moving your family, closing non-essential bank accounts, cancelling provincial health coverage, and updating all addresses to your new international location.

step.name: Phase 3: Post-Departure Compliance & Global Optimization
step.text: After departing, establish your new 'cost base' globally. The fair market value used for your Canadian deemed disposition becomes the new purchase price for those assets in your new country, which is critical to prevent double taxation. Manage any remaining Canadian assets and income, such as RRSP withdrawals (subject to non-resident withholding tax) or rental income (consider a Section 216 election to be taxed on net income). Leverage tax treaties between Canada and your new country to reduce withholding tax rates and ensure your new cost basis is legally recognized, providing the ultimate shield against double taxation.

mainEntity.name: Is my RRSP subject to the departure tax?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Your Registered Retirement Savings Plan (RRSP) is exempt from the deemed disposition rules. You will not pay capital gains tax on its value when you leave. However, any withdrawals you make as a non-resident will be subject to a Canadian withholding tax. The default rate is 25% of the gross withdrawal, though a tax treaty with your new country can often reduce this rate to 15%.

mainEntity.name: What happens to my TFSA when I leave Canada?

acceptedAnswer.text: Like an RRSP, your Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) is exempt from the departure tax. However, keeping it open is a potential trap. The tax-free status of a TFSA is generally not recognized by other countries. This means your new country will likely tax any investment gains, dividends, or interest earned within the account. The most prudent course of action is almost always to liquidate your TFSA and withdraw the funds tax-free *before* you become a non-resident.

mainEntity.name: How are stock options treated under the departure tax?

acceptedAnswer.text: The treatment is nuanced. Generally, rights under a stock option agreement are excluded from deemed disposition. However, if you exercise your options *after* becoming a non-resident, the resulting employment benefit is still considered Canadian-source income and is subject to Canadian tax, requiring you to file a Canadian return for that year. The rules differ for vested versus unvested options, making professional advice essential.

mainEntity.name: Can I avoid the departure tax?

acceptedAnswer.text: You cannot illegally evade the tax, but you can strategically mitigate or defer it. Your primary tool is to elect to defer the payment of the tax until you actually sell the assets by filing **Form T1244**. If the federal tax you are deferring is significant, you will be required to provide adequate security (such as a letter of credit) to the CRA. This strategy helps manage cash flow by aligning the tax payment with the actual cash proceeds from a future sale.

mainEntity.name: Is there an exemption from the departure tax?

acceptedAnswer.text: There is no general exemption based on net worth. However, a crucial exemption exists for **"short-term residents."** If you were a resident of Canada for 60 months or less during the 10-year period before your emigration, you are generally exempt from departure tax on any assets you owned *before* you became a resident of Canada.

mainEntity.name: Do I have to pay departure tax on my cryptocurrency?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, unequivocally. The CRA considers cryptocurrencies to be property for tax purposes. They are fully subject to the deemed disposition rules. You are treated as having sold your entire crypto portfolio at its fair market value on your departure date and must report the resulting capital gain or loss. Meticulous record-keeping of your cost base for all crypto assets is critical.

body.content: What Exactly is Canada's "Departure Tax"? (And Why It's Not a Fee to Leave)

To transform compliance anxiety into strategic control, you must first dismantle the myths. Let’s be clear: Canada's "departure tax" isn't a fee you pay at the airport for leaving the country. It is a nuanced process designed to ensure Canada collects tax on the capital gains your investments earned while you were a resident. The entire concept hinges on one critical term: **deemed disposition**.
The Core Concept: "Deemed Disposition"

Think of your departure date as a financial snapshot. On that day, the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) treats you as if you sold most of your assets at their fair market value (FMV), even though you haven't actually sold anything. This "pretend" sale is the **deemed disposition**. You then immediately "reacquire" those same assets at the new FMV. The difference between your original cost and the market value on your departure day creates a capital gain or loss, and it is on this net gain that you are taxed. This mechanism prevents the indefinite deferral of taxes on wealth accrued in Canada.
Who It Applies To

This tax applies to any individual who ceases to be a resident of Canada for tax purposes. This isn't just about your physical location; it’s about fundamentally severing your residential ties with the country. The CRA looks for a clear pattern of actions demonstrating your intent to leave permanently. Key indicators include:

items: Selling or leasing your Canadian home.
Moving your spouse, partner, and dependents with you.
Closing Canadian bank accounts and cancelling provincial health cards and driver's licenses.

body.content: Establishing residency elsewhere is the final step. You typically become a non-resident for tax purposes on the latest of the date you leave Canada, the date your family leaves, or the date you officially become a resident of your new country.
Key Assets Included vs. Exempted

The most common source of anxiety is uncertainty about which assets are affected. Understanding this distinction is the first step in building your strategic plan. The deemed disposition rule is broad, but there are several crucial exemptions.

rows: Stocks, bonds, and mutual funds in non-registered accounts
Canadian real estate (e.g., your principal residence or rental property)
Funds within a Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA)
Valuable personal property like art, jewelry, or collections
Personal-use property where the fair market value is under $10,000
Foreign property, including real estate outside of Canada
Property owned by "short-term residents" (in Canada for 60 months or less in the last 10 years) that they owned before moving to Canada

body.content: While assets like your RRSP and TFSA are exempt from the departure tax itself, they carry significant planning considerations for your life as a non-resident, which we will address.
Phase 1: Your 12-Month Pre-Departure Strategic Review

Understanding the rules is foundational, but true control comes from what you do long before your departure date. This is where you shift from a defensive taxpayer to the proactive CEO of your own emigration. A strategic exit isn't about last-minute paperwork; it's about the deliberate financial decisions you make in the year leading up to leaving. This phase is designed to give you clarity, identify risks, and unlock opportunities before they become irreversible problems.
Your first move is to build a data-driven picture of your potential departure tax liability.

items: **Conduct a "Deemed Disposition Audit"**: This is non-negotiable. Create a master ledger of every non-registered asset subject to the departure tax. For each asset—from individual stocks and crypto holdings to private company shares—document its acquisition date, its adjusted cost base (ACB), and its current fair market value. This exercise transforms vague anxiety into a concrete financial forecast and is the bedrock of your entire strategy.
**Scenario-Model Your Complex Assets**: For a global professional, the greatest risks and opportunities lie within your most complex holdings.

**Strategically Time Your Asset Sales**: Remember, "deemed" disposition is the default, not the only option. In some cases, it may be more advantageous to actually sell certain assets while you are still a Canadian resident. This allows you to utilize any available capital losses or tax credits that might not be accessible once you become a non-resident, giving you greater control over your final tax obligations.

**Decide: Pay Now or Defer?**: If the federal tax owing from your deemed disposition exceeds $16,500, you have a critical choice. You can elect to defer the payment by providing adequate security to the CRA, such as a letter of credit. This path maintains your liquidity but requires ongoing engagement with the CRA. The alternative is to pay the tax by the filing deadline—typically April 30 of the following year—which provides a clean break. Modeling the cash-flow implications of each path is an essential final step in your review.

body.content: Phase 2: The 90-Day Departure Execution Checklist

Once you've made the critical decision to pay or defer, your focus must pivot from strategic modeling to flawless execution. The final three months are about precision and diligence. This is your practical checklist to ensure a clean break, eliminating the risk of procedural errors that create long-term compliance headaches.

items: **Engage Your Professional Team**: Your Canadian accountant is only half of the equation. You must finalize your engagement with a cross-border tax professional who has deep expertise in the laws of *both* Canada and your destination country. Their role is to validate your strategy and prevent the catastrophic error of double taxation. As the cross-border financial advisors at Cerity Partners state, "Our biggest piece of advice is, before you move, learn about the financial system of your new host country... Being proactive will help you avoid costly or time-consuming mistakes."

**Obtain Formal Asset Valuations**: For any asset lacking a clear public market price, you must obtain a formal, defensible valuation. This is non-negotiable for holdings like private corporation shares or unique personal property. The CRA can and will challenge self-assessed values, and the burden of proof rests entirely on you.

**Liquidate Your TFSA (And Understand Why)**: While your Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) is exempt from the departure tax, its tax-free status is a uniquely Canadian concept. Almost no other country recognizes it. Leaving funds in your TFSA after departure creates a tax trap where future investment gains and withdrawals could become fully taxable in your new country. To ensure a clean financial break, withdraw everything from your TFSA before you sever residency.

**Prepare Your Final Filing Package**: Your final tax return is the ultimate record of your strategic exit. It will be more complex than any you have filed before. Gather the core documentation for your accountant, which will include:
**Systematically Sever Residential Ties**: Your intent to leave must be supported by clear, decisive actions. Create a checklist and execute it methodically:

body.content: Phase 3: Post-Departure Compliance & Global Optimization

Your obligations don't end when your flight departs. This final phase ensures you don't fall into the most dangerous trap for a global professional: double taxation. The meticulous work of severing ties was about closing one chapter cleanly; this stage is about opening the next one correctly.

items: **Establish Your New "Cost Base" Globally**: This is the most critical step to prevent paying tax twice on the same gain. The fair market value you used for your Canadian deemed disposition becomes the *new* cost base (or "tax basis") for those same assets in your new country. For example, if a stock was deemed "sold" at $100 per share on your Canadian return, your new country's tax authority will now consider your purchase price to be $100. When you eventually sell that stock for $120, you will only be taxed on the $20 of growth that occurred *after* you became a resident there. Meticulously document this "step-up" in basis for every asset; it is your primary defense against future double taxation.

**Manage Remaining Canadian Assets & Income**: Your emigration may not mean a complete financial separation. If you retain Canadian assets like an RRSP or rental properties, you will have ongoing tax obligations.

**Leverage Tax Treaties—Your Shield Against Double Taxation**: The mechanics of establishing a new cost basis and reducing withholding taxes are not mere policy; they are legally binding rights enshrined in tax treaties. A tax treaty is a formal agreement between two countries designed to prevent the same income from being taxed twice. This international legal framework is the ultimate backstop for your global financial strategy. It compels your new country of residence to honor the deemed disposition value from Canada as your new starting point. Without these treaties, you could be taxed in Canada on gains accrued before your departure and then taxed *again* in your new country on those very same gains. Understanding and invoking your rights under these treaties closes the loop on your strategic exit, securing your foundation for the global opportunities ahead.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
Even with a comprehensive strategy, specific questions inevitably arise. Here, we address the most common queries to provide the clarity you need to move forward with confidence.

items:
**Is my RRSP subject to the departure tax?**
    No. Your Registered Retirement Savings Plan (RRSP) is exempt from the deemed disposition rules. You will not pay capital gains tax on its value when you leave. However, any withdrawals you make as a non-resident will be subject to a Canadian withholding tax. The default rate is 25% of the gross withdrawal, though a tax treaty with your new country can often reduce this rate to 15%.

**What happens to my TFSA when I leave Canada?**
    Like an RRSP, your Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) is exempt from the departure tax. However, keeping it open is a potential trap. The tax-free status of a TFSA is generally not recognized by other countries. This means your new country will likely tax any investment gains, dividends, or interest earned within the account. The most prudent course of action is almost always to liquidate your TFSA and withdraw the funds tax-free *before* you become a non-resident.

**How are stock options treated under the departure tax?**
    The treatment is nuanced. Generally, rights under a stock option agreement are excluded from deemed disposition. However, if you exercise your options *after* becoming a non-resident, the resulting employment benefit is still considered Canadian-source income and is subject to Canadian tax, requiring you to file a Canadian return for that year. The rules differ for vested versus unvested options, making professional advice essential.

**Can I avoid the departure tax?**
    You cannot illegally evade the tax, but you can strategically mitigate or defer it. Your primary tool is to elect to defer the payment of the tax until you actually sell the assets by filing **Form T1244**. If the federal tax you are deferring is significant, you will be required to provide adequate security (such as a letter of credit) to the CRA. This strategy helps manage cash flow by aligning the tax payment with the actual cash proceeds from a future sale.

**Is there an exemption from the departure tax?**
    There is no general exemption based on net worth. However, a crucial exemption exists for **"short-term residents."** If you were a resident of Canada for 60 months or less during the 10-year period before your emigration, you are generally exempt from departure tax on any assets you owned *before* you became a resident of Canada.

**Do I have to pay departure tax on my cryptocurrency?**
    Yes, unequivocally. The CRA considers cryptocurrencies to be property for tax purposes. They are fully subject to the deemed disposition rules. You are treated as having sold your entire crypto portfolio at its fair market value on your departure date and must report the resulting capital gain or loss. Meticulous record-keeping of your cost base for all crypto assets is critical.

body.content: Conclusion: Taking Control of Your Global Journey

That final point on cryptocurrency underscores a fundamental truth: ambiguity is the enemy of confidence. Facing the complexities of the departure tax head-on is the only path to genuine peace of mind. This process doesn't have to be a barrier to your global ambitions. By reframing it as a manageable, multi-stage project, you transform yourself from a reactive taxpayer into the proactive CEO of your global career—a role you are already suited to fill. This is not merely about tax compliance; it is about taking ownership of a critical business process.
The power lies in the structure you impose on the challenge. The three-phase framework we've outlined is your executive playbook.

items: **Phase 1, the Strategic Review,** is your boardroom. It's where you move from uncertainty to clarity by auditing assets, modeling scenarios, and making data-driven decisions. This is where you trade fear for a financial forecast.

**Phase 2, Flawless Execution,** is your operations manual. It’s the 90-day sprint where strategy becomes action. By engaging professionals, securing valuations, and systematically severing ties, you build a defensible, error-free record of your departure.

**Phase 3, Global Optimization,** is your long-term value creation. This is the crucial step where you establish your new global cost basis to prevent double taxation and manage any remaining Canadian financial threads with precision.

body.content: You chose the path of the global professional for greater autonomy and control. Apply that same mindset to your departure. View this process not as a tax problem, but as the first major strategic project of your new global life. By doing so, you ensure your journey begins not with lingering uncertainty, but on a solid foundation of control, clarity, and the confidence that you have managed your exit with the rigor it deserves.
